section.title: Final Warnings and Doxology
section.sectionRationale: Before he lays down his pen, Paul sounds a last warning: watch out for those who cause divisions contrary to the teaching you have learned, for they serve their own appetites and deceive the naive. He promises that the God of peace will soon crush Satan under their feet, sends greetings from his companions, and then lifts the whole letter into a soaring doxology to the God who is able to establish His people according to the gospel and the revealed mystery, to the only wise God be glory forever through Jesus Christ.
section.thesis: The gospel that saves must be guarded against those who divide and deceive, and the God who is able to establish His people by that gospel is the God of peace who will crush the serpent and receive everlasting glory through Jesus Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements rising to a climax: a warning to watch out for and avoid those who cause divisions, with the promise that God will crush Satan; greetings from Paul's companions, including Tertius who wrote the letter down; and the closing doxology, the summit of the epistle, praising the God who establishes His people through the once hidden, now revealed gospel.

units.label: Watch out for those who cause divisions
units.note: avoid the deceivers who serve their own appetites

verses.text: Now I urge you, brothers, to watch out for those who create {{dichostasia|divisions}} and obstacles that are contrary to the teaching you have learned. Turn away from them.
For such people are not serving our Lord Christ, but their own appetites. By smooth talk and flattery they deceive the hearts of the naive.
Everyone has heard about your obedience, so I rejoice over you. But I want you to be wise about what is good and innocent about what is evil.
The God of peace will soon {{suntribo|crush}} Satan under your feet. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.

units.label: Greetings from Paul's companions
units.note: coworkers, kinsmen, and Tertius the scribe

verses.text: Timothy, my fellow worker, sends you greetings, as do Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, my fellow countrymen.
I, Tertius, who wrote down this letter, greet you in the Lord.
Gaius, who has hosted me and all the church, sends you greetings. Erastus, the city treasurer, sends you greetings, as does our brother Quartus.

units.label: The closing doxology
units.note: glory to the God who establishes us by the revealed mystery

verses.text: Now to Him who is able to strengthen you by my gospel and by the proclamation of Jesus Christ, according to the revelation of the {{mysterion|mystery}} concealed for ages past
but now revealed and made known through the writings of the prophets by the command of the eternal God, in order to lead all nations to the {{hypakoe|obedience}} that comes from faith,
to the only wise God be {{doxa|glory}} forever through Jesus Christ! Amen.

dichostasia.gloss: Division, dissension, a standing apart. Paul warns against those who create dichostasia contrary to the apostolic teaching, tearing the one body into factions. It names the work of those who wound the church's unity for the sake of their own agenda, and it is listed elsewhere among the works of the flesh.

suntribo.gloss: To crush, shatter, or break in pieces. Paul promises that the God of peace will soon suntribo Satan under the feet of His people, echoing the ancient promise that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent's head. The word carries the note of decisive, final victory over the enemy.

mysterion.gloss: A mystery, a truth once hidden in God and now disclosed. The doxology celebrates the mystery kept secret for long ages but now revealed in the gospel and made known to all nations. It is not a riddle to be solved but a secret purpose of God, unveiled in Christ, that Jew and Gentile alike are gathered into one saved people.

hypakoe.gloss: Obedience, a hearing that submits and trusts. The doxology aims at the obedience of faith among all nations, the very phrase with which the letter opened. The whole of Romans is bracketed by this goal: that the nations would come to the obedience that flows from faith in Christ.

doxa.gloss: Glory, weight, splendor, honor. The letter that began with the gospel of God ends by ascribing doxa to the only wise God forever through Jesus Christ. All the argument of Romans resolves into praise: salvation is from God and for God, and His is the everlasting glory.

basic.context: Paul closes his letter with a final pastoral warning, a round of greetings from those with him, and a magnificent doxology. Having spent the letter building up the church in the gospel, he now guards it against those who would divide and deceive it. Then, gathering up every theme of the epistle, he breaks into praise to the God who is able to establish His people by that same gospel.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the warning and the doxology together. The threat of division is real, but the last word is not danger; it is the God of peace who crushes Satan and the God of glory who establishes His people. The doxology deliberately echoes the letter's opening in Romans 1: the gospel promised through the prophets, the mystery now revealed, and the obedience of faith among all nations. It is the closing bracket of the whole book.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul warns against those who cause dichostasia, division, contrary to sound teaching. He promises that God will suntribo, crush, Satan underfoot. The doxology unveils the mysterion, the once hidden purpose of God, now revealed to bring the nations to the hypakoe of faith, the obedience Paul named in chapter one. It ends by ascribing doxa, glory, to the only wise God forever.
basic.typology: The promise that the God of peace will crush Satan under your feet reaches back to Genesis 3:15, where God foretold that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent's head. What was promised in Eden is accomplished in Christ and shared with His people. The mystery now revealed through the prophetic writings shows the whole Old Testament pointing forward to this unveiling in the gospel.
basic.christ: The doxology is offered through Jesus Christ, and the gospel it celebrates is the proclamation of Jesus Christ. He is the content of the mystery now revealed, the one in whom the nations are gathered, and the mediator through whom glory rises to God. The victory over Satan is His victory, won at the cross and shared with His people. Every strand of the letter is tied off in Him.
basic.god: The passage is God-centered from start to finish. He is the God of peace who crushes the enemy, the eternal God who commands the gospel to go to all nations, the only wise God who planned the mystery before the ages, and the God of glory who is able to establish His people. The letter that opened with the gospel of God closes with unending glory to God through Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3:15 promises that the woman's seed will crush the serpent's head, fulfilled as God crushes Satan under His people's feet. Romans 1:1-5 opens with the gospel promised through the prophets and the obedience of faith among the nations, which the doxology echoes. Ephesians 3:4-6 unfolds the mystery of Jew and Gentile made one. 1 Corinthians 14:33 calls Him the God of peace, not disorder.
basic.oneStory: The whole story of Scripture is gathered here. The serpent who struck in Eden is crushed under the feet of the redeemed. The mystery kept secret for long ages, that God would save the nations through His Son, is now revealed and preached to all peoples. The letter that opened with the gospel of God promised beforehand ends with glory to the only wise God, the story come full circle from promise to praise.

context.literary: This is the second half of the letter's closing, moving from a sharp final warning through personal greetings to the crowning doxology. The doxology is one long sentence of praise, deliberately mirroring the long sentence of the greeting in Romans 1:1-7. Together they form an inclusio, opening and closing the letter with the same great themes.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57, likely from Corinth, dictating to a scribe named Tertius who adds his own greeting in verse 22. Gaius hosts the church, and Erastus is named as the city treasurer, a notable civic official. The warning about divisive teachers reflects Paul's constant concern that smooth-talking opponents would unsettle young churches.
context.canonical: The doxology is textually notable: some manuscripts place verses 25 to 27 after chapter 14 or 15, and a few omit them, but they fittingly conclude the letter. Verse 24 is absent from the earliest manuscripts and appears in the footnotes of many editions. The doxology binds the two Testaments together, presenting the gospel as the revealed mystery long promised through the prophetic writings.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn the Roman believers against divisive, self-serving teachers and urge them to be wise and innocent; to convey greetings from his companions; and above all to close the letter with praise, gathering its whole message into a doxology to the God who establishes His people by the gospel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greetings describe Paul's circle at a moment in time. The warning and the doxology are prescriptive for the church always: guard the apostolic teaching, avoid those who divide and deceive, and give all glory to the only wise God, whose gospel establishes His people and gathers the nations to the obedience of faith.

debates.question: How should the church treat those who cause divisions contrary to sound teaching?

views.label: Mark and avoid them
views.case: Paul says plainly to watch out for them and turn away, refusing them a platform, since they serve their own appetites and deceive the naive with smooth talk; the church protects its unity and its members by not entertaining such teaching.

views.label: Engage and correct patiently first
views.case: Others stress that elsewhere Paul urges gentle correction of opponents in hope of repentance, so avoidance follows only after patient warning has failed; the aim is always the health of the body and, if possible, the recovery of the erring.

debates.bearing: Both agree the divisive teaching must not be allowed to spread and that the flock must be guarded; the historic pastoral practice is to warn clearly, seek repentance, and separate from those who persist in dividing the church against the apostolic gospel.

debates.question: Where do the verses of the doxology belong, given the manuscript variations?

views.label: The original close of the letter
views.case: The best reading places verses 25 to 27 here as the fitting climax, echoing the opening of Romans and rounding off the epistle with praise; this is where most manuscripts and modern editions set them.

views.label: A moveable ending in some copies
views.case: Some manuscripts locate the doxology after chapter 14 or 15, likely reflecting early liturgical or editorial use, and a few omit it; but this does not unsettle its authenticity or its fitness as the letter's conclusion.

debates.bearing: However the copies arrange it, the doxology is received as the true and fitting end of Romans, gathering the letter's themes into glory to God through Jesus Christ; the variation touches placement, not the gospel it proclaims.

study.originalLanguages: The final movement turns on a handful of charged words. Paul warns against those who sow dichostasia, division, against the teaching the church has received. He promises that the God of peace will soon suntribo, crush, Satan underfoot, a word of decisive victory that carries the echo of Eden. The doxology then unveils the mysterion, the secret purpose of God hidden for ages and now revealed, so that the nations might come to the hypakoe of faith, the same obedience named in Romans 1. And it closes by lifting doxa, glory, to the only wise God forever through Jesus Christ.

typology.type: The seed of the woman crushing the serpent
typology.fulfillment: Genesis 3:15 is answered as the God of peace crushes Satan under the feet of Christ's people, the victory of Christ shared with His church.

typology.type: The mystery hidden in the prophets
typology.fulfillment: The prophetic writings pointed forward to a purpose kept secret for ages, now revealed in the gospel: the nations gathered into one saved people through Christ.
The promise that all peoples would be blessed and would obey reaches its goal as the gospel brings the nations to the obedience of faith.

study.christ: The doxology rises through Jesus Christ and celebrates the proclamation of Jesus Christ. He is the mystery now made known, the one in whom the nations are gathered and reconciled, and the mediator through whom all glory ascends to God. The crushing of Satan is His triumph, won at the cross and empty tomb, now shared with those who are in Him. The letter that has been unfolding the riches of Christ from the first verse ends by giving Him the honor of the everlasting doxology.
study.god: God fills the horizon of these closing verses. He is the God of peace who shatters the great enemy, the eternal God whose command sends the gospel to every nation, the only wise God whose hidden purpose is now revealed, and the God who alone is able to establish His people. The whole letter has been about what God has done in Christ, and it ends where it must, in glory to God forever.

crossReferences.note: The first promise that the woman's seed would crush the serpent's head, fulfilled as God crushes Satan under His people's feet.
The letter opens with the gospel promised through the prophets and the obedience of faith among the nations, which the doxology echoes and completes.
The mystery of Christ, that Gentiles are fellow heirs with Israel, now revealed as it is celebrated in the doxology.
God is the God of peace and not of disorder, the same title Paul invokes against those who cause division.

study.oneStory: The letter ends by drawing the whole biblical story to a point. The serpent who struck in the garden is crushed beneath the feet of the redeemed. The purpose God kept secret through long ages, to save the nations through His Son, is now unveiled and preached to all peoples, bringing them to the obedience of faith. What opened as the gospel of God promised beforehand closes as glory to the only wise God through Jesus Christ, the story carried from ancient promise to unending praise.

quiz.q: What does Paul tell the Romans to do about those who cause divisions contrary to sound teaching?

options.text: Watch out for them and turn away from them, for they serve their own appetites.
options.note: Paul urges vigilance and avoidance, since such people deceive the naive and serve themselves, not Christ.

options.text: Appoint them as leaders to keep the peace.
options.note: Paul says to avoid them, not to give them authority.

options.text: Ignore them entirely and never warn anyone.
options.note: Paul does say to turn away, but not to stay silent; he warns the church clearly before telling them to avoid such people.

options.text: Adopt their teaching to preserve unity.
options.note: Unity is a real concern, but their teaching is contrary to what the church learned; adopting it would be the very division Paul warns against.

quiz.q: What does Paul promise the God of peace will soon do?

options.text: Crush Satan under the feet of His people.
options.note: Paul promises decisive victory, echoing the promise of Genesis 3:15.

options.text: Remove all hardship from their lives at once.
options.note: God does bring peace and victory, but the specific promise here is the crushing of Satan, not the immediate end of all trials.

options.text: Leave the enemy undefeated forever.
options.note: This concerns Satan, but says the opposite of the promise: God will crush him underfoot.

options.text: Reward the divisive teachers.
options.note: God crushes the enemy; He does not reward those who serve their own appetites.

quiz.q: Who is Tertius, mentioned in the greetings?

options.text: The scribe who wrote down the letter as Paul dictated it.
options.note: Tertius identifies himself as the one who wrote the letter down, adding his own greeting in the Lord.

options.text: The city treasurer of Corinth.
options.note: That is Erastus, also named in the greetings; Tertius is the one who wrote the letter down.

options.text: The founder of the Roman church.
options.note: No single person is credited with founding the Roman church; Tertius is the scribe, not its founder.

options.text: A false teacher Paul is warning against.
options.note: Tertius is a brother greeting the church in the Lord, not an opponent.

quiz.q: In the doxology, how is the gospel described in relation to the ages past?

options.text: As a mystery concealed for ages but now revealed and made known to all nations.
options.note: The doxology celebrates the once hidden mystery now disclosed through the prophetic writings to the nations.

options.text: As a brand new idea with no roots in the Scriptures.
options.note: It is made known through the writings of the prophets; it is not rootless.

options.text: As a secret that remains hidden from everyone.
options.note: The mystery is now revealed and made known to all nations.

options.text: As a message only for one nation.
options.note: It is now revealed, but made known to all nations to bring the obedience of faith, not to one nation only.

quiz.q: How does the letter finally end, and to whom is glory given?

options.text: To the only wise God be glory forever through Jesus Christ.
options.note: The doxology ascribes everlasting glory to the only wise God through Jesus Christ.

options.text: To the Roman church, for its faithfulness.
options.note: Paul does rejoice in their obedience, but the glory is given to God alone, not to the church.

options.text: To Paul, for writing such a great letter.
options.note: Paul is the writer, but he directs all glory to God, not to himself.

options.text: To the wisdom of the age.
options.note: Glory is given to the only wise God, not to human or worldly wisdom.

questions: Paul warns against those who cause divisions with smooth talk and flattery. How can you grow more discerning, wise about what is good and innocent about what is evil, without becoming cynical?
The God of peace promises to crush Satan under His people's feet. How does this echo of Eden's promise strengthen you when you feel the enemy's pressure?
Tertius, the scribe, and many unnamed helpers made this letter possible. Where might God be calling you to serve faithfully in a supporting role that others may never notice?
The doxology celebrates a mystery once hidden but now revealed to all nations. How does it move you to know that God planned your salvation before the ages and has now made it known?
The letter ends with glory to God forever through Jesus Christ. How would ordering your life around the glory of God, rather than your own, reshape your priorities this week?

translationNotes.note: The promise that the God of peace will crush Satan under your feet deliberately echoes Genesis 3:15, the promise that the woman's seed would crush the serpent's head, now shared with Christ's people.
The earliest manuscripts do not include verse 24, a repeated grace benediction; it appears in the footnotes of many editions and is omitted here, so the numbering moves from verse 23 to verse 25.

The obedience that comes from faith renders the same phrase found in Romans 1:5, framing the whole letter. The sentence of the doxology runs on into verse 27, and an em dash in some editions is rendered here as a comma to avoid the dash while keeping the flow.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 16:17-27. Footnote markers were omitted, line breaks rendered as single spaces, and the em dash at the end of verse 26 was rendered as a comma to preserve the running sentence into verse 27. Verse 24 is absent from the earliest manuscripts and is not included, so numbering runs 23 then 25. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
